commit 6d8c1f80f02bfbdc0ebdcd561737528114573bc4
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()

This commit moves the end_pgoff calculation before next_uptodate_folio() to
avoid nr_pages calculation overflows during filemap_map_pages(). By getting
the file size earlier, it attempts to keep the retrieved folio consistent
with the file end.

> --- a/mm/filemap.c
> +++ b/mm/filemap.c
> @@ -3884,14 +3884,19 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
>  	unsigned int nr_pages = 0, folio_type;
>  	unsigned short mmap_miss = 0, mmap_miss_saved;
>  
> +	/*
> +	 * Recalculate end_pgoff based on file_end before calling
> +	 * next_uptodate_folio() to avoid races with concurrent
> +	 * truncation.
> +	 */
> +	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;

Does this code underflow file_end when the file is empty?

If i_size_read(mapping->host) returns 0, DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) evaluates
to 0. Since pgoff_t is unsigned, 0 - 1 underflows to ULONG_MAX.

If the file is concurrently extended and a folio is added to the page cache,
next_uptodate_folio() will return it. Could the unconstrained file_end then
bypass boundary checks, causing the code to map the entire folio and grant
userspace access to memory beyond EOF without triggering SIGBUS?

> +	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
> +
>  	rcu_read_lock();
>  	folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff);

Can this introduce a time-of-check to time-of-use race with file truncation?

By caching file_end before the folio is locked in next_uptodate_folio(), a
concurrent truncation could occur after file_end is calculated but before the
folio is locked.

During truncation, truncate_pagecache() may unmap a straddling large folio to
enforce SIGBUS semantics while leaving it in the page cache. Could
filemap_map_pages() then lock the folio and use the stale, larger file_end to
incorrectly re-establish PTEs or PMDs for the unmapped pages, bypassing
SIGBUS?

>  	if (!folio)
>  		goto out;
>  
> -	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
> -	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
> -
>  	/*
>  	 * Do not allow to map with PMD across i_size to preserve
>  	 * SIGBUS semantics.

Target Commit:
commit 6d8c1f80f02bfbdc0ebdcd561737528114573bc4
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 17 09:29:55 2026 +0000

    mm: filemap: fix nr_pages calculation overflow in filemap_map_pages()
    
    When running stress-ng on my Arm64 machine with v7.0-rc3 kernel, I encountered
    some very strange crash issues showing up as "Bad page state":
    
    "
    [  734.496287] BUG: Bad page state in process stress-ng-env  pfn:415735fb
    [  734.496427] page: refcount:0 mapcount:1 mapping:0000000000000000 index:0x4cf316 pfn:0x415735fb
    [  734.496434] flags: 0x57fffe000000800(owner_2|node=1|zone=2|lastcpupid=0x3ffff)
    [  734.496439] raw: 057fffe000000800 0000000000000000 dead000000000122 0000000000000000
    [  734.496440] raw: 00000000004cf316 0000000000000000 0000000000000000 0000000000000000
    [  734.496442] page dumped because: nonzero mapcount
    "
    
    After analyzing this page’s state, it is hard to understand why the mapcount
    is not 0 while the refcount is 0, since this page is not where the issue first
    occurred. By enabling the CONFIG_DEBUG_VM config, I can reproduce the crash as
    well and captured the first warning where the issue appears:
    
    "
    [  734.469226] page: refcount:33 mapcount:0 mapping:00000000bef2d187 index:0x81a0 pfn:0x415735c0
    [  734.469304] head: order:5 mapcount:0 entire_mapcount:0 nr_pages_mapped:0 pincount:0
    [  734.469315] memcg:ffff000807a8ec00
    [  734.469320] aops:ext4_da_aops ino:100b6f dentry name(?):"stress-ng-mmaptorture-9397-0-2736200540"
    [  734.469335] flags: 0x57fffe400000069(locked|uptodate|lru|head|node=1|zone=2|lastcpupid=0x3ffff)
    ......
    [  734.469364] page dumped because: VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO((_Generic((page + nr_pages - 1),
    const struct page *: (const struct folio *)_compound_head(page + nr_pages - 1), struct page *:
    (struct folio *)_compound_head(page + nr_pages - 1))) != folio)
    [  734.469390] ------------[ cut here ]------------
    [  734.469393] WARNING: ./include/linux/rmap.h:351 at folio_add_file_rmap_ptes+0x3b8/0x468,
    CPU#90: stress-ng-mlock/9430
    [  734.469551]  folio_add_file_rmap_ptes+0x3b8/0x468 (P)
    [  734.469555]  set_pte_range+0xd8/0x2f8
    [  734.469566]  filemap_map_folio_range+0x190/0x400
    [  734.469579]  filemap_map_pages+0x348/0x638
    [  734.469583]  do_fault_around+0x140/0x198
    ......
    [  734.469640]  el0t_64_sync+0x184/0x188
    "
    
    The code that triggers the warning is: "VM_WARN_ON_FOLIO(page_folio(page + nr_pages - 1) != folio, folio)",
    which indicates that set_pte_range() tried to map beyond the large folio’s
    size.
    
    By adding more debug information, I found that 'nr_pages' had overflowed in
    filemap_map_pages(), causing set_pte_range() to establish mappings for a range
    exceeding the folio size, potentially corrupting fields of pages that do not
    belong to this folio (e.g., page->_mapcount).
    
    After above analysis, I think the possible race is as follows:
    
    CPU 0                                                  CPU 1
    filemap_map_pages()                                   ext4_setattr()
       //get and lock folio with old inode->i_size
       next_uptodate_folio()
    
                                                              .......
                                                              //shrink the inode->i_size
                                                              i_size_write(inode, attr->ia_size);
    
       //calculate the end_pgoff with the new inode->i_size
       file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
       end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
    
       ......
       //nr_pages can be overflowed, cause xas.xa_index > end_pgoff
       end = folio_next_index(folio) - 1;
       nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1;
    
       ......
       //map large folio
       filemap_map_folio_range()
                                                              ......
                                                              //truncate folios
                                                              truncate_pagecache(inode, inode->i_size);
    
    To fix this issue, move the 'end_pgoff' calculation before next_uptodate_folio(),
    so the retrieved folio stays consistent with the file end to avoid 'nr_pages'
    calculation overflow. After this patch, the crash issue is gone.
    
    Fixes: 743a2753a02e ("filemap: cap PTE range to be created to allowed zero fill in folio_map_range()")
    Reported-by: Yuanhe Shu <xiangzao@linux.alibaba.com>
    Tested-by: Yuanhe Shu <xiangzao@linux.alibaba.com>
    Acked-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
    Signed-off-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

diff --git a/mm/filemap.c b/mm/filemap.c
index 242c80a0d12d..2b933a1da9bd 100644
--- a/mm/filemap.c
+++ b/mm/filemap.c
@@ -3884,14 +3884,19 @@ vm_fault_t filemap_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
 	unsigned int nr_pages = 0, folio_type;
 	unsigned short mmap_miss = 0, mmap_miss_saved;
 
+	/*
+	 * Recalculate end_pgoff based on file_end before calling
+	 * next_uptodate_folio() to avoid races with concurrent
+	 * truncation.
+	 */
+	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
+	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
+
 	rcu_read_lock();
 	folio = next_uptodate_folio(&xas, mapping, end_pgoff);
 	if (!folio)
 		goto out;
 
-	file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;
-	end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
-
 	/*
 	 * Do not allow to map with PMD across i_size to preserve
 	 * SIGBUS semantics.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Filemap Map Pages Vulnerability**

Okay, so I've been digging into this `filemap_map_pages()` issue, trying to understand this integer overflow. It seems that the root cause boils down to a race condition where the file's size (`i_size`) can be truncated *concurrently* while pages are being mapped. The original code's logic has a window where an outdated file size can be used, leading to an incorrect `nr_pages` calculation and, ultimately, out-of-bounds PTE mappings.

The key flaw is how `file_end` and `end_pgoff` are calculated and used with `next_uptodate_folio()`. Initially, the code fetches a folio, *then* calculates the end page offset (`file_end` and `end_pgoff`) based on the file size. This means the *first* call to `next_uptodate_folio()` could use an end offset based on the *original* file size. This is the problem.

Now, if a concurrent truncate happens between fetching the first folio and the `min` calculations, `end_pgoff` can be reduced. However, the subsequent calls to `next_uptodate_folio()` use the potentially *smaller* end offset. This creates a situation where the initial `xas.xa_index` might be beyond this now smaller `end_pgoff`. This is the crux. The critical point is: if `end_pgoff` is reduced and becomes smaller than `xas.xa_index`, then a negative number becomes the result of `end_pgoff - xas.xa_index`, that's an unsigned integer, and so it wraps to a huge number for `nr_pages`. BOOM! That explains the crash.

The proposed fix seems conceptually correct: the calculation of `file_end` and `end_pgoff` is moved *before* the first call to `next_uptodate_folio()`. This ensures that `end_pgoff` reflects the current, *potentially* truncated file size before any folios are even accessed. By making sure that `end_pgoff` is consistent throughout the loop, `xas.xa_index` will never exceed the updated `end_pgoff`. So, `nr_pages` won't overflow.

However, there's always a "however," right? I need to be sure. I'm thinking, what if a *second* truncation occurs mid-mapping? But wait, the `file_end` and `end_pgoff` values are calculated only once at the beginning. That means, if `xas.xa_index` is being found, it can't exceed that first calculation and so it's safe. It seems robust *because* the end offset is fixed. Okay. Also, since `next_uptodate_folio()` returns a *locked* folio, this protects against the truncate path.

There's another edge case: what about a file that's truncated *to zero*? The patch now potentially results in `file_end` being set to `ULONG_MAX` (due to how unsigned integer math works with `DIV_ROUND_UP`). But, if the file is zero size, we *shouldn't* map anything! I need to trace through the logic. Wait, if `i_size` is zero, any page in the pagecache is about to be truncated. So, even though, mathematically, `file_end` ends up being `ULONG_MAX`, the code *should* be safe because the truncate will unmap them.

There's one more edge case: what if `start_pgoff` is greater than the newly calculated `end_pgoff`? If `next_uptodate_folio()` gets an `end_pgoff` smaller than `start_pgoff`, it immediately returns `NULL`. It's safe!

So, yes, it all looks pretty good. This fix appears to address the vulnerability, preventing the integer overflow by correctly bounding the folio mapping within the current file size. I think this is it!

page_cache_delete(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\t   struct folio *folio, void *shadow)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, folio->index);\n\tlong nr = 1;\n\n\tmapping_set_update(&xas, filemap_unaccount_folio(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tstruct folio *folio)\n{\n\tlong nr;\n\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_mapped(folio), folio);\n\tif (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DEBUG_VM) && unlikely(folio_mapped(folio))) {\n\t\tpr_alert(\"BUG: Bad page cache in process %s  pfn:%05lx\\n\",\n\t\t\t current->comm, folio_pfn(folio));\n\t\tdump_page(&folio->page, \"still mapped when (folio_test_pmd_mappable(folio))\n\t\t\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_SHMEM_THPS, -nr);\n\t} else if (folio_test_pmd_mappable(folio)) {\n\t\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_FILE_THPS, -nr);\n\t\tfilemap_nr_thps_dec(mapping);\n\t}\n\tif (test_bit(AS_KERNEL_FILE, &folio->mapping->flags))\n\t\tmod_node_page_state(folio_pgdat(folio),\n\t\t\t\t    NR_KERNEL_FILE_PAGES, -nr);\n\n\t/*\n\t * At this point folio must be either written or cleaned by\n\t * truncate.  Dirty folio here signals a bug and loss of\n\t * unwritten data - on ordinary filesystems.\n\t *\n\t * But it's harmless on in-memory filesystems like tmpfs; and can\n\t * occur when a driver which did get_user_pages() sets page dirty\n\t * before putting it, while the inode is being finally evicted.\n\t *\n\t * Below fixes dirty accounting after removing the folio entirely\n\t * but leaves the dirty flag set: it has no effect for truncated\n\t * folio and anyway will be cleared before returning folio to\n\t * buddy allocator.\n\t */\n\tif (WARN_ON_ONCE(folio_test_dirty(folio) &&\n\t\t\t mapping_can_writeback(mapping)))\n\t\tfolio_account_cleaned(folio, inode_to_wb(mapping->host));\n}\n\n/*\n * Delete a page from the page cache and free it. Caller has to make\n * sure the page is locked and that nobody else uses it - or that usage\n * is safe.     "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-only\n/*\n *\tlinux/mm/filemap.c\n *\n * Copyright (C) 1994-1999  Linus Torvalds\n */\n\n/*\n * This file handles the generic file mmap semantics used by\n * most \"normal\" filesystems (but you don't /have/ to use this:\n * the NFS filesystem used to do this differently, for example)\n */\n#include <linux/export.h>\n#include <linux/compiler.h>\n#include <linux/dax.h>\n#include <linux/fs.h>\n#include <linux/sched/signal.h>\n#include <linux/uaccess.h>\n#include <linux/capability.h>\n#include <linux/kernel_stat.h>\n#include <linux/gfp.h>\n#include <linux/mm.h>\n#include <linux/swap.h>\n#include <linux/leafops.h>\n#include <linux/syscalls.h>\n#include <linux/mman.h>\n#include <linux/pagemap.h>\n#include <linux/file.h>\n#include <linux/uio.h>\n#include <linux/error-injection.h>\n#include <linux/hash.h>\n#include <linux/writeback.h>\n#include <linux/backing-dev.h>\n#include <linux/folio_batch.h>\n#include <linux/security.h>\n#include <linux/cpuset.h>\n#include <linux/hugetlb.h>\n#include <linux/memcontrol.h>\n#include <linux/shmem_fs.h>\n#include <linux/rmap.h>\n#include <linux/delayacct.h>\n#include <linux/psi.h>\n#include <linux/ramfs.h>\n#include <linux/page_idle.h>\n#include <linux/migrate.h>\n#include <linux/pipe_fs_i.h>\n#include <linux/splice.h>\n#include <linux/rcupdate_wait.h>\n#include <linux/sched/mm.h>\n#include <linux/sysctl.h>\n#include <linux/pgalloc.h>\n\n#include <asm/tlbflush.h>\n#include \"internal.h\"\n\n#define CREATE_TRACE_POINTS\n#include <trace/events/filemap.h>\n\n/*\n * FIXME: remove all knowledge of the buffer layer from the core VM\n */\n#include <linux/buffer_head.h> /* for try_to_free_buffers */\n\n#include <asm/mman.h>\n\n#include \"swap.h\"\n\n/*\n * Shared mappings implemented 30.11.1994. It's not fully working yet,\n * though.\n *\n * Shared mappings now work. 15.8.1995  Bruno.\n *\n * finished 'unifying' the page and buffer cache and SMP-threaded the\n * page-cache, 21.05.1999, Ingo Molnar <mingo@redhat.com>\n *\n * SMP-threaded pagemap-LRU 1999, Andrea Arcangeli <andrea@suse.de>\n */\n\n/*\n * Lock ordering:\n *\n *  ->i_mmap_rwsem\t\t(truncate_pagecache)\n *    ->private_lock\t\t(__free_pte->block_dirty_folio)\n *      ->swap_lock\t\t(exclusive_swap_page, others)\n *        ->i_pages lock\n *\n *  ->i_rwsem\n *    ->invalidate_lock\t\t(acquired by fs in truncate path)\n *      ->i_mmap_rwsem\t\t(truncate->unmap_mapping_range)\n *\n *  ->mmap_lock\n *    ->i_mmap_rwsem\n *      ->page_table_lock or pte_lock\t(various, mainly in memory.c)\n *        ->i_pages lock\t(arch-dependent flush_dcache_mmap_lock)\n *\n *  ->mmap_lock\n *    ->invalidate_lock\t\t(filemap_fault)\n *      ->lock_page\t\t(filemap_fault, access_process_vm)\n *\n *  ->i_rwsem\t\t\t(generic_perform_write)\n *    ->mmap_lock\t\t(fault_in_readable->do_page_fault)\n *\n *  bdi->wb.list_lock\n *    sb_lock\t\t\t(fs/fs-writeback.c)\n *    ->i_pages lock\t\t(__sync_single_inode)\n *\n *  ->i_mmap_rwsem\n *    ->anon_vma.lock\t\t(vma_merge)\n *\n *  ->anon_vma.lock\n *    ->page_table_lock or pte_lock\t(anon_vma_prepare and various)\n *\n *  ->page_table_lock or pte_lock\n *    ->swap_lock\t\t(try_to_unmap_one)\n *    ->private_lock\t\t(try_to_unmap_one)\n *    ->i_pages lock\t\t(try_to_unmap_one)\n *    ->lruvec->lru_lock\t(follow_page_mask->mark_page_accessed)\n *    ->lruvec->lru_lock\t(check_pte_range->folio_isolate_lru)\n *    ->private_lock\t\t(folio_remove_rmap_pte->set_page_dirty)\n *    ->i_pages lock\t\t(folio_remove_rmap_pte->set_page_dirty)\n *    bdi.wb->list_lock\t\t(folio_remove_rmap_pte->set_page_dirty)\n *    ->inode->i_lock\t\t(folio_remove_rmap_pte->set_page_dirty)\n *    bdi.wb->list_lock\t\t(zap_pte_range->set_page_dirty)\n *    ->inode->i_lock\t\t(zap_pte_range->set_page_dirty)\n *    ->private_lock\t\t(zap_pte_range->block_dirty_folio)\n */\n\nstatic void page_cache_delete(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\t   struct folio *folio, void *shadow)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, folio->index);\n\tlong nr = 1;\n\n\tmapping_set_update(&xas, mapping);\n\n\txas_set_order(&xas, folio->index, folio_order(folio));\n\tnr = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(!folio_test_locked(folio), folio);\n\n\txas_store(&xas, shadow);\n\txas_init_marks(&xas);\n\n\tfolio->mapping = NULL;\n\t/* Leave folio->index set: truncation lookup relies upon it */\n\tmapping->nrpages -= nr;\n}\n\nstatic void filemap_unaccount_folio(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tstruct folio *folio)\n{\n\tlong nr;\n\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_mapped(folio), folio);\n\tif (!IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DEBUG_VM) && unlikely(folio_mapped(folio))) {\n\t\tpr_alert(\"BUG: Bad page cache in process %s  pfn:%05lx\\n\",\n\t\t\t current->comm, folio_pfn(folio));\n\t\tdump_page(&folio->page, \"still mapped when deleted\");\n\t\tdump_stack();\n\t\tadd_taint(TAINT_BAD_PAGE, LOCKDEP_NOW_UNRELIABLE);\n\n\t\tif (mapping_exiting(mapping) && !folio_test_large(folio)) {\n\t\t\tint mapcount = folio_mapcount(folio);\n\n\t\t\tif (folio_ref_count(folio) >= mapcount + 2) {\n\t\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t\t * All vmas have already been torn down, so it's\n\t\t\t\t * a good bet that actually the page is unmapped\n\t\t\t\t * and we'd rather not leak it: if we're wrong,\n\t\t\t\t * another bad page check should catch it later.\n\t\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t\tatomic_set(&folio->_mapcount, -1);\n\t\t\t\tfolio_ref_sub(folio, mapcount);\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\t/* hugetlb folios do not participate in page cache accounting. */\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tnr = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\n\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_FILE_PAGES, -nr);\n\tif (folio_test_swapbacked(folio)) {\n\t\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_SHMEM, -nr);\n\t\tif (folio_test_pmd_mappable(folio))\n\t\t\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_SHMEM_THPS, -nr);\n\t} else if (folio_test_pmd_mappable(folio)) {\n\t\tlruvec_stat_mod_folio(folio, NR_FILE_THPS, -nr);\n\t\tfilemap_nr_thps_dec(mapping);\n\t}\n\tif (test_bit(AS_KERNEL_FILE, &folio->mapping->flags))\n\t\tmod_node_page_state(folio_pgdat(folio),\n\t\t\t\t    NR_KERNEL_FILE_PAGES, -nr);\n\n\t/*\n\t * At this point folio must be either written or cleaned by\n\t * truncate.  Dirty folio here signals a bug and loss of\n\t * unwritten data - on ordinary filesystems.\n\t *\n\t * But it's harmless on in-memory filesystems like tmpfs; and can\n\t * occur when a driver which did get_user_pages() sets page dirty\n\t * before putting it, while the inode is being finally evicted.\n\t *\n\t * Below fixes dirty accounting after removing the folio entirely\n\t * but leaves the dirty flag set: it has no effect for truncated\n\t * folio and anyway will be cleared before returning folio to\n\t * buddy allocator.\n\t */\n\tif (WARN_ON_ONCE(folio_test_dirty(folio) &&\n\t\t\t mapping_can_writeback(mapping)))\n\t\tfolio_account_cleaned(folio, inode_to_wb(mapping->host));\n}\n\n/*\n * Delete a page from the page cache and free it. Caller has to make\n * sure the page is locked and that nobody else uses it - or that usage\n * is safe.  The caller must hold the i_pages lock.\n */\nvoid __filemap_remove_folio(struct folio *folio, void *shadow)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = folio->mapping;\n\n\ttrace_mm_filemap_delete_from_page_cache(folio);\n\tfilemap_unaccount_folio(mapping, folio);\n\tpage_cache_delete(mapping, folio, shadow);\n}\n\nvoid filemap_free_folio(struct address_space *mapping, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tvoid (*free_folio)(struct folio *);\n\n\tfree_folio = mapping->a_ops->free_folio;\n\tif (free_folio)\n\t\tfree_folio(folio);\n\n\tfolio_put_refs(folio, folio_nr_pages(folio));\n}\n\n/**\n * filemap_remove_folio - Remove folio from page cache.\n * @folio: The folio.\n *\n * This must be called only on folios that are locked and have been\n * verified to be in the page cache.  It will never put the folio into\n * the free list because the caller has a reference on the page.\n */\nvoid filemap_remove_folio(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = folio->mapping;\n\n\tBUG_ON(!folio_test_locked(folio));\n\tspin_lock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\txa_lock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\t__filemap_remove_folio(folio, NULL);\n\txa_unlock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\tif (mapping_shrinkable(mapping))\n\t\tinode_lru_list_add(mapping->host);\n\tspin_unlock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\n\tfilemap_free_folio(mapping, folio);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_FOR_MODULES(filemap_remove_folio, \"kvm\");\n\n/*\n * page_cache_delete_batch - delete several folios from page cache\n * @mapping: the mapping to which folios belong\n * @fbatch: batch of folios to delete\n *\n * The function walks over mapping->i_pages and removes folios passed in\n * @fbatch from the mapping. The function expects @fbatch to be sorted\n * by page index and is optimised for it to be dense.\n * It tolerates holes in @fbatch (mapping entries at those indices are not\n * modified).\n *\n * The function expects the i_pages lock to be held.\n */\nstatic void page_cache_delete_batch(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t     struct folio_batch *fbatch)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, fbatch->folios[0]->index);\n\tlong total_pages = 0;\n\tint i = 0;\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\tmapping_set_update(&xas, mapping);\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, ULONG_MAX) {\n\t\tif (i >= folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\n\t\t/* A swap/dax/shadow entry got inserted? Skip it. */\n\t\tif (xa_is_value(folio))\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * A page got inserted in our range? Skip it. We have our\n\t\t * pages locked so they are protected from being removed.\n\t\t * If we see a page whose index is higher than ours, it\n\t\t * means our page has been removed, which shouldn't be\n\t\t * possible because we're holding the PageLock.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (folio != fbatch->folios[i]) {\n\t\t\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio->index >\n\t\t\t\t\tfbatch->folios[i]->index, folio);\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tWARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));\n\n\t\tfolio->mapping = NULL;\n\t\t/* Leave folio->index set: truncation lookup relies on it */\n\n\t\ti++;\n\t\txas_store(&xas, NULL);\n\t\ttotal_pages += folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\t}\n\tmapping->nrpages -= total_pages;\n}\n\nvoid delete_from_page_cache_batch(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\t\t\t  struct folio_batch *fbatch)\n{\n\tint i;\n\n\tif (!folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tspin_lock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\txa_lock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\tfor (i = 0; i < folio_batch_count(fbatch); i++) {\n\t\tstruct folio *folio = fbatch->folios[i];\n\n\t\ttrace_mm_filemap_delete_from_page_cache(folio);\n\t\tfilemap_unaccount_folio(mapping, folio);\n\t}\n\tpage_cache_delete_batch(mapping, fbatch);\n\txa_unlock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\tif (mapping_shrinkable(mapping))\n\t\tinode_lru_list_add(mapping->host);\n\tspin_unlock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\n\tfor (i = 0; i < folio_batch_count(fbatch); i++)\n\t\tfilemap_free_folio(mapping, fbatch->folios[i]);\n}\n\nint filemap_check_errors(struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tint ret = 0;\n\t/* Check for outstanding write errors */\n\tif (test_bit(AS_ENOSPC, &mapping->flags) &&\n\t    test_and_clear_bit(AS_ENOSPC, &mapping->flags))\n\t\tret = -ENOSPC;\n\tif (test_bit(AS_EIO, &mapping->flags) &&\n\t    test_and_clear_bit(AS_EIO, &mapping->flags))\n\t\tret = -EIO;\n\treturn ret;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(filemap_check_errors);\n\nstatic int filemap_check_and_keep_errors(struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\t/* Check for outstanding write errors */\n\tif (test_bit(AS_EIO, &mapping->flags))\n\t\treturn -EIO;\n\tif (test_bit(AS_ENOSPC, &mapping->flags))\n\t\treturn -ENOSPC;\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic int filemap_writeback(struct address_space *mapping, loff_t start,\n\t\tloff_t end, enum writeback_sync_modes sync_mode,\n\t\tlong *nr_to_write)\n{\n\tstruct writeback_control wbc = {\n\t\t.sync_mode\t= sync_mode,\n\t\t.nr_to_write\t= nr_to_write ? *nr_to_write : LONG_MAX,\n\t\t.range_start\t= start,\n\t\t.range_end\t= end,\n\t};\n\tint ret;\n\n\tif (!mapping_can_writeback(mapping) ||\n\t    !mapping_tagged(mapping, PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\twbc_attach_fdatawrite_inode(&wbc, mapping->host);\n\tret = do_writepages(mapping, &wbc);\n\twbc_detach_inode(&wbc);\n\n\tif (!ret && nr_to_write)\n\t\t*nr_to_write = wbc.nr_to_write;\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n/**\n * filemap_fdatawrite_range - start writeback on mapping dirty pages in range\n * @mapping:\taddress space structure to write\n * @start:\toffset in bytes where the range starts\n * @end:\toffset in bytes where the range ends (inclusive)\n\n... [Diff truncated. Dropped 3952 lines] ...\n\n\t\tcond_resched();\n\n\t\tif (unlikely(status == 0)) {\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * A short copy made ->write_end() reject the\n\t\t\t * thing entirely.  Might be memory poisoning\n\t\t\t * halfway through, might be a race with munmap,\n\t\t\t * might be severe memory pressure.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tif (chunk > PAGE_SIZE)\n\t\t\t\tchunk /= 2;\n\t\t\tif (copied) {\n\t\t\t\tbytes = copied;\n\t\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * 'folio' is now unlocked and faults on it can be\n\t\t\t * handled. Ensure forward progress by trying to\n\t\t\t * fault it in now.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tif (fault_in_iov_iter_readable(i, bytes) == bytes) {\n\t\t\t\tstatus = -EFAULT;\n\t\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tpos += status;\n\t\t\twritten += status;\n\t\t}\n\t} while (iov_iter_count(i));\n\n\tif (!written)\n\t\treturn status;\n\tiocb->ki_pos += written;\n\treturn written;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_perform_write);\n\n/**\n * __generic_file_write_iter - write data to a file\n * @iocb:\tIO state structure (file, offset, etc.)\n * @from:\tiov_iter with data to write\n *\n * This function does all the work needed for actually writing data to a\n * file. It does all basic checks, removes SUID from the file, updates\n * modification times and calls proper subroutines depending on whether we\n * do direct IO or a standard buffered write.\n *\n * It expects i_rwsem to be grabbed unless we work on a block device or similar\n * object which does not need locking at all.\n *\n * This function does *not* take care of syncing data in case of O_SYNC write.\n * A caller has to handle it. This is mainly due to the fact that we want to\n * avoid syncing under i_rwsem.\n *\n * Return:\n * * number of bytes written, even for truncated writes\n * * negative error code if no data has been written at all\n */\nssize_t __generic_file_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = file->f_mapping;\n\tstruct inode *inode = mapping->host;\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tret = file_remove_privs(file);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\n\tret = file_update_time(file);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\treturn ret;\n\n\tif (iocb->ki_flags & IOCB_DIRECT) {\n\t\tret = generic_file_direct_write(iocb, from);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * If the write stopped short of completing, fall back to\n\t\t * buffered writes.  Some filesystems do this for writes to\n\t\t * holes, for example.  For DAX files, a buffered write will\n\t\t * not succeed (even if it did, DAX does not handle dirty\n\t\t * page-cache pages correctly).\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (ret < 0 || !iov_iter_count(from) || IS_DAX(inode))\n\t\t\treturn ret;\n\t\treturn direct_write_fallback(iocb, from, ret,\n\t\t\t\tgeneric_perform_write(iocb, from));\n\t}\n\n\treturn generic_perform_write(iocb, from);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(__generic_file_write_iter);\n\n/**\n * generic_file_write_iter - write data to a file\n * @iocb:\tIO state structure\n * @from:\tiov_iter with data to write\n *\n * This is a wrapper around __generic_file_write_iter() to be used by most\n * filesystems. It takes care of syncing the file in case of O_SYNC file\n * and acquires i_rwsem as needed.\n * Return:\n * * negative error code if no data has been written at all of\n *   vfs_fsync_range() failed for a synchronous write\n * * number of bytes written, even for truncated writes\n */\nssize_t generic_file_write_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *from)\n{\n\tstruct file *file = iocb->ki_filp;\n\tstruct inode *inode = file->f_mapping->host;\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tinode_lock(inode);\n\tret = generic_write_checks(iocb, from);\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = __generic_file_write_iter(iocb, from);\n\tinode_unlock(inode);\n\n\tif (ret > 0)\n\t\tret = generic_write_sync(iocb, ret);\n\treturn ret;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(generic_file_write_iter);\n\n/**\n * filemap_release_folio() - Release fs-specific metadata on a folio.\n * @folio: The folio which the kernel is trying to free.\n * @gfp: Memory allocation flags (and I/O mode).\n *\n * The address_space is trying to release any data attached to a folio\n * (presumably at folio->private).\n *\n * This will also be called if the private_2 flag is set on a page,\n * indicating that the folio has other metadata associated with it.\n *\n * The @gfp argument specifies whether I/O may be performed to release\n * this page (__GFP_IO), and whether the call may block\n * (__GFP_RECLAIM & __GFP_FS).\n *\n * Return: %true if the release was successful, otherwise %false.\n */\nbool filemap_release_folio(struct folio *folio, gfp_t gfp)\n{\n\tstruct address_space * const mapping = folio->mapping;\n\n\tBUG_ON(!folio_test_locked(folio));\n\tif (!folio_needs_release(folio))\n\t\treturn true;\n\tif (folio_test_writeback(folio))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (mapping && mapping->a_ops->release_folio)\n\t\treturn mapping->a_ops->release_folio(folio, gfp);\n\treturn try_to_free_buffers(folio);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(filemap_release_folio);\n\n/**\n * filemap_invalidate_inode - Invalidate/forcibly write back a range of an inode's pagecache\n * @inode: The inode to flush\n * @flush: Set to write back rather than simply invalidate.\n * @start: First byte to in range.\n * @end: Last byte in range (inclusive), or LLONG_MAX for everything from start\n *       onwards.\n *\n * Invalidate all the folios on an inode that contribute to the specified\n * range, possibly writing them back first.  Whilst the operation is\n * undertaken, the invalidate lock is held to prevent new folios from being\n * installed.\n */\nint filemap_invalidate_inode(struct inode *inode, bool flush,\n\t\t\t     loff_t start, loff_t end)\n{\n\tstruct address_space *mapping = inode->i_mapping;\n\tpgoff_t first = start >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tpgoff_t last = end >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tpgoff_t nr = end == LLONG_MAX ? ULONG_MAX : last - first + 1;\n\n\tif (!mapping || !mapping->nrpages || end < start)\n\t\tgoto out;\n\n\t/* Prevent new folios from being added to the inode. */\n\tfilemap_invalidate_lock(mapping);\n\n\tif (!mapping->nrpages)\n\t\tgoto unlock;\n\n\tunmap_mapping_pages(mapping, first, nr, false);\n\n\t/* Write back the data if we're asked to. */\n\tif (flush)\n\t\tfilemap_fdatawrite_range(mapping, start, end);\n\n\t/* Wait for writeback to complete on all folios and discard. */\n\tinvalidate_inode_pages2_range(mapping, start / PAGE_SIZE, end / PAGE_SIZE);\n\nunlock:\n\tfilemap_invalidate_unlock(mapping);\nout:\n\treturn filemap_check_errors(mapping);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(filemap_invalidate_inode);\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_CACHESTAT_SYSCALL\n/**\n * filemap_cachestat() - compute the page cache statistics of a mapping\n * @mapping:\tThe mapping to compute the statistics for.\n * @first_index:\tThe starting page cache index.\n * @last_index:\tThe final page index (inclusive).\n * @cs:\tthe cachestat struct to write the result to.\n *\n * This will query the page cache statistics of a mapping in the\n * page range of [first_index, last_index] (inclusive). The statistics\n * queried include: number of dirty pages, number of pages marked for\n * writeback, and the number of (recently) evicted pages.\n */\nstatic void filemap_cachestat(struct address_space *mapping,\n\t\tpgoff_t first_index, pgoff_t last_index, struct cachestat *cs)\n{\n\tXA_STATE(xas, &mapping->i_pages, first_index);\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\n\t/* Flush stats (and potentially sleep) outside the RCU read section. */\n\tmem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited(NULL);\n\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\txas_for_each(&xas, folio, last_index) {\n\t\tint order;\n\t\tunsigned long nr_pages;\n\t\tpgoff_t folio_first_index, folio_last_index;\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Don't deref the folio. The primary concern is ensuring that we don't map pages beyond the file size, especially when concurrent truncation is happening. I need to make sure the fix is correct, and I'm looking at potential race conditions introduced by the patch.

First, I realize there's a quick check at the beginning, but if `start_pgoff > end_pgoff`, the whole loop just returns `NULL`, so there's no mapping. That’s because `xas_next_entry` returns `NULL` immediately if the index is beyond the maximum.

I’m looking at how the truncation and faultaround interact, particularly focusing on how `end_pgoff` (representing the VMA end or a file size cap) is calculated and used relative to `i_size`. The patch recalculates `end_pgoff` *before* calling `next_uptodate_folio` to avoid races with truncation. This is the core of the fix. I think it is important because the critical bit is calculating and checking `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(...), PAGE_SIZE) - 1`. If `i_size` shrinks between the initial `i_size_read` and mapping, there is a possibility that pages beyond the new file size are mapped.

The critical issue is the `i_size = 0` case. If `i_size` shrinks to 0 *after* the initial `filemap_fault` (which returns SIGBUS if `offset >= i_size`), and we get here, `file_end` becomes `ULONG_MAX`. This is an underflow, and so we essentially lose the clamping to the file size. I think this can lead to mapping beyond what should be mapped. If `i_size == 0`, accessing the region would normally SIGBUS.

If `i_size` is already 0 at the time of the initial `filemap_fault`, then faultaround isn't triggered, because `filemap_fault` returns SIGBUS, so we don't end up in `filemap_map_pages`.

I'm checking: will `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` have an issue? When `i_size == 0`, `file_end = ULONG_MAX`. Now, what if `i_size` *shrinks* to 0 *after* the fault check in `filemap_fault`? We end up in `filemap_map_pages` now with `file_end = ULONG_MAX`. Is this a problem?

The concern is if `i_size` shrinks to 0 *concurrently* with mapping. If we map a folio, for example, that should have been unmapped because it now exceeds `i_size = 0`. Since `i_size` is 0, by POSIX we MUST SIGBUS on access. However, because the folio *was* present in page cache, `filemap_map_pages` can actually map pages beyond `i_size`. Since the `truncate` is *running*, and the pages were in the cache, we map for a short window, the `truncate_pagecache` unmaps it, and things are fine (mostly).

It seems this is an *existing*, *unfixed* bug with a small race window. If the file shrinks to 0 bytes and there are existing folios still in page cache, we map pages beyond EOF in a very narrow window and they are unmapped later. But wait, `filemap_map_pages` is doing this FOR FAULTAROUND! The actual fault address was already checked against `i_size` in `filemap_fault`.

If `i_size` *concurrently* shrinks to 0, `filemap_map_pages` maps the pages (possibly PMD). The `truncate_pagecache` process unmaps the pages, and for a short time the faulting process might get old data, which is actually fine because it already passed the check in `filemap_fault` that the access was within the *original* file size.

It means that there is a discontinuity in how we process a file of 0 bytes vs. 1 byte. Shrinking to 1 byte, we correctly clamp the size. Shrinking to 0 bytes, we do not, because the `- 1` underflows. But it's an existing bug in file system drivers that's not introduced by the patch.

However, I started wondering if there were cases where a zero `i_size` could cause problems, especially with the ordering of checks for `i_size`. The old code had a safety net, it would seem, where clamping was performed *before* checking if any pages existed. But it looks like, under certain circumstances, a zero-size file could lead to an unlimited number of pages being mapped. Turns out this wasn't an issue because an empty file just returned NULL. So this wasn't a problem.

The problem, however, lies in how the `file_end` is calculated relative to where a folio is returned by `next_uptodate_folio`. It appears that if `i_size` *increases* between the `file_end` calculation and the `next_uptodate_folio` call, we have a significant problem.

Let me explain the race:

*   **CPU 0** reads the `i_size` as zero, calculates `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX`.
*   **CPU 0** then clamps `end_pgoff` with `file_end` but sees no effect
*   **CPU 1** extends the `i_size` to some positive value (e.g., 10 pages).
*   **CPU 1** writes data and a large folio is allocated with the data.
*   **CPU 0**, running concurrently, calls `next_uptodate_folio` *after* the `i_size` is increased. So `next_uptodate_folio` picks up the increase in size, but because `file_end` was `ULONG_MAX` previously, `end_pgoff` remains the large value, the VMA's end, and `nr_pages` is now calculated based on this large value.
*   **CPU 0** then *incorrectly* maps pages beyond the actual new `i_size`. Say, it's 16 pages, while the file's size is 10 pages.
*   **Userspace** accesses a page *beyond* the intended `i_size` (e.g., page 11).

Here's the problem: The current standard says that if a page is accessed that is beyond `DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size, PAGE_SIZE)`, the standard mandates SIGBUS behavior. However, this race installs PTEs beyond that boundary. The process doesn't SIGBUS because the PTE is installed. This violates POSIX SIGBUS semantics. Accesses should be triggering SIGBUS, and they aren't! This is not acceptable.

So, this looks like a definite race condition. The fact that `do_fault_around` *unconditionally* calls `map_pages` opens up the window for this race, even if an initial size is zero. The fault path does not check `i_size` before the `map_pages` call.

**Tracing the Page Fault Journey**

Okay, so the fundamental thing to grasp is that in most filesystems, `filemap_map_pages` is the function that gets hooked up to the `.map_pages` field. This means it's the workhorse that handles mapping pages when a page fault occurs. I'm primarily concerned with how it fits into the Virtual Memory (VM) subsystem, specifically where it's called.

Essentially, the VM's `do_fault_around` function is the point of interest. It's the one that's going to trigger the call to `map_pages`. The path of execution is what is of interest. My next steps are going to involve tracing exactly how `do_fault_around` operates and how that eventually triggers a call to `map_pages`. I need to see the context, the conditions, and the data it passes along. This will reveal the mechanics of page fault handling in this specific filesystem scenario.

    "content": "{"content":"mm/memory.c-5771- * It uses vm_ops->map_pages() to map the pages, which skips the page if it's\nmm/memory.c-5772- * not ready to be mapped: not up-to-date, locked, etc.\nmm/memory.c-5773- *\nmm/memory.c-5774- * This function doesn't cross VMA or page table boundaries, in order to call\nmm/memory.c-5775- * map_pages() and acquire a PTE lock only once.\nmm/memory.c-5776- *\nmm/memory.c-5777- * fault_around_pages defines how many pages we'll try to map.\nmm/memory.c-5778- * do_fault_around() expects it to be set to a power of two less than or equal\nmm/memory.c-5779- * to PTRS_PER_PTE.\nmm/memory.c-5780- *\nmm/memory.c-5781- * The virtual address of the area that we map is naturally aligned to\nmm/memory.c-5782- * fault_around_pages * PAGE_SIZE rounded down to the machine page size\nmm/memory.c-5783- * (and therefore to page order).  This way it's easier to guarantee\nmm/memory.c-5784- * that we don't cross page table boundaries.\nmm/memory.c-5785- */\nmm/memory.c:5786:static vm_fault_t do_fault_around(struct vm_fault *vmf)\nmm/memory.c-5787-{\nmm/memory.c-5788-\tpgoff_t nr_pages = READ_ONCE(fault_around_pages);\nmm/memory.c-5789-\tpgoff_t pte_off = pte_index(vmf->address);\nmm/memory.c-5790-\t/* The page offset of vmf->address within the VMA. */\nmm/memory.c-5791-\tpgoff_t vma_off = vmf->pgoff - vmf->vma->vm_pgoff;\nmm/memory.c-5792-\tpgoff_t from_pte, to_pte;\nmm/memory.c-5793-\tvm_fault_t ret;\nmm/memory.c-5794-\nmm/memory.c-5795-\t/* The PTE offset of the start address, clamped to the VMA. */\nmm/memory.c-5796-\tfrom_pte = max(ALIGN_DOWN(pte_off, nr_pages),\nmm/memory.c-5797-\t\t       pte_off - min(pte_off, vma_off));\nmm/memory.c-5798-\nmm/memory.c-5799-\t/* The PTE offset of the end address, clamped to the VMA and PTE. */\nmm/memory.c-5800-\tto_pte = min3(from_pte + nr_pages, (pgoff_t)PTRS_PER_PTE,\nmm/memory.c-5801-\t\t      pte_off + vma_pages(vmf->vma) - vma_off) - 1;\n"}",

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of `filemap_map_pages` and Potential Issues**

Okay, let's break down this `do_fault_around` function and the `filemap_map_pages` call. It calculates a range of page indices and calls into `vm_ops->map_pages`, which itself calls `filemap_map_pages`. The primary concern here is the lock ordering, especially within `filemap_map_pages`. I see that it uses `rcu_read_lock()` at the start. Then, for the folios, there's `folio_trylock()` called within `next_uptodate_folio()`. The page table lock (`ptl`) is acquired later via `pte_offset_map_lock`, but after `next_uptodate_folio()` already returns.

Initially, I was worried about whether `folio_trylock()` could be called under the `ptl`, but since `trylock` is non-blocking, it's valid. The crucial aspect is that the page table lock (`ptl`) is held throughout the entire loop, across calls to `next_uptodate_folio()` and the folio lock operations, which *is* valid. The `rcu_read_lock()` is also held for the entire duration, and that is also valid since folio_trylock doesn't sleep.

Now, let's get into the calculation of `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;`. In the original code, this was computed *after* the initial `folio` was obtained from `next_uptodate_folio()`, and I had a question about moving this call before, and whether it could introduce a problem if `i_size` is 0.

I've confirmed that `mapping->host` is the inode and thus not NULL in this context. But now, here's where things get interesting. What if `i_size` is 0? The key is that `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` results in `ULONG_MAX` due to integer underflow. In the *original* code, if `i_size` was initially 0, `next_uptodate_folio` would have returned `NULL` and the `file_end` calculation wouldn't have even happened. In this new arrangement, that's not the case. This means the code could be doing things *it never did before*.

So, if `i_size` is 0, the code now calculates `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX`, and `next_uptodate_folio` will still return `NULL`. Is this *safe*? The crucial question is: if `i_size` is *later* truncated to 0, what happens? If the size changes after `next_uptodate_folio` is called, but before it completes, is the change in the file size accounted for?

I've realized that the real danger lies in the case where `i_size` is *initially* > 0, but is then truncated to 0 *during* the loop. If `i_size` *grows* after `next_uptodate_folio` is called, all the folios will be considered valid and mapped, and the code behaves correctly. However, a shrinking `i_size` can now cause problems the *previous* code did not have!

However, I've realized that this *underflow to `ULONG_MAX`* is something that was always possible, regardless of the patch! If `i_size` *was* truncated to 0 immediately after `next_uptodate_folio`, the previous code would have also resulted in the folio being mapped! This behavior has always been possible, and now this patch is making the possibility more obvious.

Therefore, this patch doesn't *introduce* a new problem, but it does make an existing one more obvious.

    "thought": "**Analysis of a Potential SIGBUS Violation in File Mapping**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial instinct was right to be wary; the documentation highlights specific boundary checks related to large folios and `i_size`. The issue hinges on a patch that moves the `file_end` calculation earlier in `filemap_map_pages`, before critical synchronization points.

The core of the problem seems to be an integer underflow. If the initial file size (`i_size`) is zero, then `file_end` is calculated as `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1`, resulting in `ULONG_MAX`.  Essentially, the check against `i_size` is effectively disabled because any folio's index will always be less than `ULONG_MAX`. If the file then *grows* after the `file_end` computation, but *before*  `next_uptodate_folio`, we have a dangerous window.

Specifically, a thread might initialize `file_end` with an `i_size` of zero. Later, another thread modifies `i_size` to some non-zero value and the filesystem populates the page cache with a large folio.  When the first thread finally reaches `next_uptodate_folio`, the large folio is discovered, and because `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, the code incorrectly *PMD-maps* this entire folio.

The problem? We're potentially mapping a huge chunk of memory that *should* trigger a SIGBUS if accessed, because it's beyond the current `i_size`.  The system is circumventing POSIX standards by silently providing zero-filled pages.  This breaks the expected SIGBUS behavior and opens a potential security hole.  An attacker could create an empty file, map a large region, write some data, then grow the file.  The read after the grow should SIGBUS but instead succeeds, as the full folio is already mapped into the VMA.

The pre-patch behavior was fine because the `file_end` would have been recalculated *after* the first `next_uptodate_folio` call, so the `i_size` would have been respected at every step. This patch changed that by moving computation of `file_end` before the lock, creating this race.

It's not just PMD mapping either. Even with PTE mapping, the `nr_pages` calculation is affected, potentially mapping pages beyond the true `i_size`. Therefore, a simple file truncation followed by a `mmap` could make the program vulnerable by not providing SIGBUS for pages that are out of bounds.

The fix, therefore, is to move the computation of `file_end` *after* the read lock. This ensures that the code uses the most recent `i_size` when calculating the upper bound of the file mapping. Any other approach will introduce a bug that can be exploited in a variety of ways.

    "thought": "**Analysis of File Mapping and SIGBUS Vulnerability**

Okay, so here's the situation as I see it. I've been digging into how page faults are handled in the kernel, specifically focusing on the `filemap_fault` and `filemap_map_pages` functions, and I think I've found a critical bug related to file size changes and SIGBUS signals.

The core of the problem lies in how `file_end` is calculated and used in conjunction with `filemap_map_pages`, especially when a file's size grows from zero. Basically, when `file_end` becomes `ULONG_MAX` due to a file being initially empty, and then grows, this leads to an over-mapping of pages.

Here's how it plays out: The check `index >= max_idx` in `filemap_fault` returns SIGBUS for faults out of the file size. When `i_size` is zero at the start, `file_end` is set to `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1`, which is `ULONG_MAX`. When pages are mapped in `filemap_map_pages`, `file_end` is not used properly, causing an over-mapping. If `i_size` then grows, the previously over-mapped pages are now *within* the file's bounds, according to the kernel, and the `SIGBUS` check never happens. Thus an out of bounds read, past the files current size, will succeed rather than generating a SIGBUS.

Specifically, the "fault around" optimization, which uses `filemap_map_pages`, is where the vulnerability arises. This function aims to map pages surrounding a faulting address. If the target page isn't mapped, it calls `filemap_fault`, which *should* trigger a `SIGBUS` if the access is beyond the file size.

But, if the file starts empty (i.e. `i_size` is zero), `file_end` becomes `ULONG_MAX`. The function then maps pages around the fault address. If the file size grows *after* that mapping happens, but *before* the user accesses a page, the check in `filemap_fault` is bypassed because `filemap_map_pages` has already mapped the pages. This means the user program will **not** receive a `SIGBUS` when accessing pages beyond the now-valid `i_size`.

Even if the page tables were constructed correctly at the time of the `do_fault_around` call, they will persist until the next unmap, at which point the kernel might not invalidate the large pages.

This affects both PMD and PTE mappings. Because the `file_end` value is underflowing to `ULONG_MAX` when `i_size` is zero, there is an over-mapping, and therefore no SIGBUS generated. The size of these mappings is capped by a check on `file_end`. Since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, the pages are allowed to be mapped.

The patch I'm looking at moved a calculation, which *seemed* like it was fixing an underflow. But I now see it's only *moving* it. The problem is that the `file_end` calculation uses the initial, zero `i_size`. When that `i_size` grows *later*, the over-mapping persists. This means there is no check on page access beyond what would otherwise be permitted.

I've confirmed that `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1` results in `ULONG_MAX`. This is the fundamental issue.

The fix I've thought of is to properly handle the case where `i_size` is zero, not to map anything or compute `file_end` as `0`.

Essentially, the fix is to *check* the `max_idx` value, and if it's zero, *skip* the mapping steps.

I've also examined whether a *shrink* in file size during the process could be problematic. While a shrink is concurrent, it unmaps and updates `i_size` after `filemap_map_pages` executes, preventing issues. However, growth *after* an initial zero size is the real issue. In that case the original PTE's will remain valid, which is the problem.

In summary, this is a serious vulnerability, where a file initially of zero size and subsequently growing will cause a POSIX SIGBUS signal to *not* be triggered. The kernel will read out of bounds. The standard approach to this is to prevent the mapping if `i_size` is zero.

Alright, let's break this down. My initial assessment correctly identified a potential integer underflow issue, and after diving deeper, it's pretty clear the patch introduces a significant vulnerability. My main concern lies in the unconditional calculation of `file_end` before properly checking file size. This is where things get interesting. The core of the problem stems from the underflow of `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1`, resulting in `ULONG_MAX` for empty files. Previously, because `file_end` was computed *after* we successfully found a folio using `next_uptodate_folio()`, this calculation was skipped for empty files and we remained safe. Now, because this is computed first, when `i_size` is zero, we get an incorrect `file_end`.

Here's the chain reaction:

1.  We have `file_end = ULONG_MAX`, essentially bypassing any `i_size` constraints.
2.  Now, the subsequent calculation `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end)` makes `end_pgoff` unconstrained.
3.  A concurrent thread then extends the file, say to 100 pages. The page cache is populated.
4.  Because we can find a large folio after the extension, and due to `file_end` being `ULONG_MAX`, the code incorrectly maps the *entire* folio, potentially PMD mapping many more pages than exist in the file. `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` is always true, and the over-mapping occurs.
5.  Since the file was extended, the correct `truncate_pagecache()` call will *not* immediately unmap the over-mapped pages. This is because `truncate_pagecache` will only consider the *new* size of the file, so it does not unmap pages before the extension. The pages beyond 100 will persist after the extension.

Consequently, this creates a SIGBUS hole, allowing userspace processes to illegally read or write beyond the effective EOF without any SIGBUS protection, a clear violation of POSIX semantics. This vulnerability wasn't present before the patch due to the conditional `file_end` calculation. The fix would involve checking if `i_size` is zero before calculating `file_end`. The current design exposes a potential security risk, allowing userspace to bypass the standard EOF checks when a file is extended from being empty. I verified the reasoning, checked for the lock order, and validated the flow. The underlying problem is the TOCTOU (time-of-check to time-of-use) race condition. The `file_end` is calculated when the file is empty, then the file is extended. The check `file_end >= folio_next_index` allows mapping beyond the end of file.

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that's supposed to fix a potential `nr_pages` calculation overflow in `filemap_map_pages()`. The original code calculates `end_pgoff` *after* calling `next_uptodate_folio()`, and the patch moves the calculation *before*. The commit message says this avoids an "nr_pages calculation overflow" by ensuring consistency between the retrieved folio and the file end. My initial thought is, "Wait, is this really the best approach?" What if `i_size_read(mapping->host)` changes concurrently?

Looking at the loop, `end_pgoff` is used to cap `xas.xa_index` within the `next_uptodate_folio` function call. So if we update it earlier, the returned folios stay within bounds. Okay, that makes sense on the surface. But let's dig deeper. The loop condition itself relies on `next_uptodate_folio`, which also takes `end_pgoff`. So the change appears to be correct.

Now, let's consider a scenario where `i_size_read` is zero. In this case, `file_end` becomes `DIV_ROUND_UP(0, PAGE_SIZE) - 1`, which evaluates to `0 - 1 = ULONG_MAX` (assuming pgoff_t is unsigned long). That's a red flag! An empty file should have `file_end` as -1 (but since it's unsigned, this becomes ULONG_MAX). In the original code, the calculation gives a `file_end` of ULONG_MAX. So, `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end)` will just be `end_pgoff`.

If the file is empty, does the call to `next_uptodate_folio` return something? If the file was just truncated, there may still be folios in the page cache temporarily (or a folio was found). If `end_pgoff` is `ULONG_MAX`, the `nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1;` calculation just uses `end`. And the original code also calculates `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX` in the case of `i_size` being zero. It appears there is no real fix here.

Let's look at how `end_pgoff` is used in `next_uptodate_folio`. If `i_size_read` is 0, `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, and `end_pgoff` remains the value it was (e.g., `start_pgoff + 15`). In this case, `next_uptodate_folio` returns a folio. So, we'd have `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` which might be true since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`. This is how you'd map a PMD beyond EOF! So the patch doesn't fix it. It seems we need to re-evaluate the calculation. The `file_end` is wrong.

The issue stems from the order of operations when a file is truncated to zero. The `i_size` is updated *before* the associated pages are removed from the page cache. This opens a small but significant window for a race condition. If a read fault happens concurrently with the truncate, `filemap_map_pages` can be called *before* the pagecache is fully truncated.

Here's the breakdown of my concerns:

1.  **The Flawed `file_end` Calculation**: The code computes `file_end` like this: `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;`. When `i_size` is zero, `file_end` underflows to `ULONG_MAX`. This is an unsigned integer underflow.
2.  **`end_pgoff` Bound**: The computed `file_end` is used to limit `end_pgoff`. Specifically, `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end)`. However, since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX` when `i_size` is 0, this does *not* effectively limit `end_pgoff`. Thus, `end_pgoff` is unconstrained.
3.  **`next_uptodate_folio` Traversal**: The next step calls `next_uptodate_folio`, which searches for folios in the pagecache. If the file is being truncated to 0, this lookup *might* find a folio because the page cache hasn't been cleared yet.
4.  **The Inconsistent Mapping Logic**: Here's where it gets really bad. The `file_end` value of `ULONG_MAX` completely subverts the intent of the check. The key conditional is:
    ```c
    if ((file_end >= folio_next_index(folio) || shmem_mapping(mapping)) &&
        filemap_map_pmd(vmf, folio, start_pgoff)) {
    ```
    Since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, the check is always true and the entire large folio will be mapped with the current PMD. This is intended to be protected by SIGBUS, which the patch is designed to protect.
    Specifically, it's called in `filemap_map_folio_range` and it completely circumvents the bounds check, potentially mapping *way* beyond the intended file size. We should hit SIGBUS, but we don't.
5.  **Data Exposure**: Because the code calculates `file_end` as `ULONG_MAX`, the mapping code *thinks* that the entire folio is within the file size, even though it's *not* during a race with truncate to 0. This means we're exposing potentially old data that should have been invalidated and that we might not be supposed to have in the VMA mapping. The behavior is inconsistent: When shrunk to 1 page, we respect the `file_end`. But when shrunk to 0, we pretend `file_end == ULONG_MAX` and map everything!
6.  **The Fix and Verification**: The fix should involve a more robust calculation of `file_end`:
    ```c
    unsigned long max_idx = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE);

    if (unlikely(!max_idx))
    	goto out;
    file_end = max_idx - 1;
    ```
    This corrects the underflow issue by using the page index, or by using,
    ```c
    pgoff_t file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE);

    if (unlikely(!file_end))
    	goto out;
    file_end--;
    ```
    This avoids the underflow if `i_size` is 0.

So, in essence, the integer underflow caused by a zero file size bypasses the intended boundaries, leading to incorrect mapping and the risk of userspace reading stale data and breaking SIGBUS semantics for zeroed files. I'm confident that these fixes will prevent the incorrect mapping beyond EOF.

The primary problem is how `file_end` is calculated: it uses `DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE) - 1`.

Now, here's where it goes south. When `i_size_read()` returns 0 (which can happen with a concurrent `truncate(0)`), `DIV_ROUND_UP` results in 0. Subtracting 1 from 0 in *unsigned* arithmetic leads to `ULONG_MAX`. So `file_end` becomes `ULONG_MAX`.  Consequently, `end_pgoff` which has been set to the minimum value between its original value and `file_end`, is basically left unconstrained (because any `min` with `ULONG_MAX` gives the original value).

This is the killer: the code, if it *finds* a folio in the page cache, will happily proceed to map it!  The subsequent check `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` is where this explodes. Since `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, this check *always* passes, even if the folio's index is far beyond the *actual* file size (which is 0).

The consequence is dire: `filemap_map_pages` and its subroutines will *incorrectly* map memory, even for a zero-sized file, by essentially ignoring the fact that the `i_size` is zero. This happens because the integer underflow on the `file_end` calculation results in the logical bound check for the file to be ignored.

The logic bug stems from incorrect calculation of the `file_end` value when the file is empty: If `file_end` is `ULONG_MAX`, the check against the end of the file is always false, no matter how many pages are actually allocated to the file, and is an unintended consequence of unsigned arithmetic. Thus, there is the potential to map pages even when the file size is zero. This opens a nasty security hole by allowing access to memory outside the intended file bounds, which is definitely a violation of the expected SIGBUS behavior, and needs to be addressed.

The fix I propose is to short-circuit the entire process when the number of file pages is 0, so, before we do *anything*, let's add a check:

```c
    pgoff_t file_pages = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(mapping->host), PAGE_SIZE);

    if (unlikely(!file_pages))
        goto out;

    file_end = file_pages - 1;
    end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end);
```

This prevents the underflow and ensures that if the file is zero-sized, we simply return without attempting to map any pages.

I have verified the code for various conditions, and it is a safe fix for the described vulnerability.

Alright, let's break this down. My initial concern was about the `next_uptodate_folio()` function correctly validating `max_idx` itself. However, the patch's core change, moving the `end_pgoff = min(end_pgoff, file_end)` calculation before the `next_uptodate_folio` call within `filemap_map_pages`, warrants a deeper look. The key question: is `file_end` still valid *inside* `filemap_map_pages`, specifically in `filemap_map_folio_range`, and the answer is yes, `filemap_map_folio_range` receives this value.

The vulnerability stems from a race condition during concurrent truncation. If `i_size` shrinks *between* when a folio is checked with `next_uptodate_folio` and the `end_pgoff` calculation, `end_pgoff` could become smaller than the folio's starting index (`xas.xa_index`). `next_uptodate_folio` identifies a folio at `xas.xa_index`, but the subsequent `file_end` calculation could indicate a smaller file size than the folio size. The resulting `nr_pages` calculation then underflows because `end_pgoff - xas.xa_index` becomes negative, which is treated as a massive unsigned integer, and this integer causes `set_pte_range()` to go out of bounds! This is the core issue that the patch addresses: `nr_pages` underflows because `end_pgoff` drops below `xas.xa_index`.

The patch fixes this by calculating `file_end` and updating `end_pgoff` *before* `next_uptodate_folio`. If the file size shrinks *after* `file_end` is calculated, there are two key safeguards. First, `next_uptodate_folio` checks its own internal `max_idx` based on the *current* `i_size` inside its loop. Second, it checks `folio->mapping != mapping` which handles actual truncation of the folio. If `i_size` shrinks after `file_end` is calculated, then `next_uptodate_folio` might see the new smaller `i_size`. Now if `next_uptodate_folio` sees the smaller `i_size`, it will return `NULL` or loop. The key is what if the folio is locked first? If `i_size` shrinks *before* `i_size_read` in `next_uptodate_folio`, it skips the folio. However, if the locked folio is returned by `next_uptodate_folio`, what happens if `i_size` shrinks after `next_uptodate_folio`? Then `end_pgoff` has been calculated based on the OLD (larger) `i_size`. So the `nr_pages = min(end, end_pgoff) - xas.xa_index + 1` is now strictly positive, and `nr_pages` won't underflow.

The key to preventing data corruption here is understanding that the truncation needs the folio lock!
If `i_size` shrinks, *before* we lock the folio, `next_uptodate_folio`'s `i_size_read` will detect the smaller size and reject it (`goto unlock;`). If `i_size` shrinks *after* `next_uptodate_folio`'s `i_size_read`, then the folio is ALREADY locked by `next_uptodate_folio`. The key is that `truncate_pagecache` updates `i_size` first and only then locks and truncates pages:
1. `i_size_write(inode, newsize);`
2. `truncate_pagecache(inode, newsize);` -> locks folio.
If `filemap_map_pages` locks the folio FIRST, then `ext4_setattr` updates `i_size_write`, then `truncate_pagecache` blocks on the folio lock. Then, `filemap_map_pages` safely maps the folio into page tables.
So the only problem was that `file_end` was read *after* locking the folio, meaning that the `file_end` read could reflect the *new* smaller `i_size` while processing a folio that was locked *before* the `i_size` changed, leading to `end_pgoff < xas.xa_index`.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Potential Race Condition During File Size Shrinkage and `mmap` Operations**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and trying to see if there's a potential issue where a file shrinks concurrently with an `mmap` operation. The core of the problem seems to be how `filemap_map_pages` handles changes to `i_size` (the file size) during its execution, specifically when `file_end` is calculated *before* the loop and therefore might be stale.

My concern is that if the file shrinks *after* `filemap_map_pages` calculates `file_end`, but *before* the folios are mapped, there could be a race. Specifically:

1.  `file_end` is initialized to the old file size.
2.  `end_pgoff` (which I also calculate before the loop) is determined using `file_end`.
3.  The loop, driven by `next_uptodate_folio`, iterates over folios up to `end_pgoff`. Critically, `next_uptodate_folio` *does* check the current `i_size` under the folio lock.
4.  If the file shrinks in the middle, `next_uptodate_folio` *should* return the folio if it's still within the new, smaller `i_size`.
5.  But, the problem lies in `filemap_map_folio_range`. It still uses that *stale* `file_end`, the original larger file size. And here, I check if `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)`. If it is, and this condition is still true, the folio *could* be mapped as a PMD even though the *current* `i_size` is smaller!

The thing that has me worried is that large folios might be mapped as PMDs, potentially crossing `i_size`, which is a violation of POSIX `SIGBUS` semantics.

Let me break down the scenario:

*   The file initially has a size (e.g., 100 pages). `filemap_map_pages` starts, calculates `file_end` as 100 and `end_pgoff` as 100 as well.
*   Concurrently, the file shrinks to 50 pages (via `i_size_write`).
*   `filemap_map_pages` is in its loop. `next_uptodate_folio` is called. It *reads* the new file size (50) under the folio lock. It successfully returns folios that are still inside the new file size (within 50).
*   However, `filemap_map_folio_range` is called with the *old* `file_end` (100).
*   Even though `next_uptodate_folio` correctly checks the *current* size, `filemap_map_folio_range` uses the outdated `file_end`. Thus, I may attempt to map the folio pages, possibly as a PMD, to an area that *was* in the file but is now beyond the actual size.

Now, if `truncate_pagecache` is called, it will unmap pages *beyond* the current `i_size`. So, is this a problem? The VFS *should* unmap beyond `i_size`.

If `truncate_inode_pages_range` is reached during a concurrent truncation:

*   It zeroes the partial folio, because that's what it should do.
*   But is the folio split? Is the PMD unmapped?

`truncate_cleanup_folio` unmaps the folio, and calls `truncate_inode_partial_folio()` only for a partial folio.
So, `truncate_inode_partial_folio` is the key. Does it split or unmap the PMD?

The worry is the following.

*   `filemap_map_folio_range` maps a folio as a PMD to an area beyond the *current* `i_size` using the old `file_end`.
*   The partial folio is zeroed by the truncation process but *not* unmapped if it is part of a PMD.
*   This would lead to a user seeing *zeroes* instead of the correct `SIGBUS` upon access.

My immediate question is: does `truncate_inode_partial_folio` unmap the PMD? From my understanding of `split_huge_page()`, yes, it *does* unmap the PMD by calling `unmap_page()`, which subsequently drops the PMD mapping. But... what happens if the split *fails*? It seems like it's left in the page cache!

Here's the problem: if it's left in the page cache, and `filemap_map_pages` had already mapped it as a PMD *before* the truncation call, then we'd have a PMD that covers more than `i_size`. Is that PMD properly unmapped in these scenarios?

I vaguely recall `try_to_unmap()` being called during `split_huge_page()`. However, if the split fails (say, due to an `EBUSY` error), the mapping *might* not be unmapped.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential SIGBUS Bug in File Mapping with Shrinking Files**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `filemap_map_folio_range` function, and there's a critical check in the code:

```c
	if ((file_end >= folio_next_index(folio) || shmem_mapping(mapping)) &&
```

Right, if that first condition, `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)`, is true, the kernel goes ahead and installs a PMD mapping. The problem I see is that if the file's `i_size` shrinks, `file_end` – which, I remember, is calculated *once* before the loop in `filemap_map_pages` – might still be *greater than or equal to* `folio_next_index(folio)`. So, the PMD mapping WILL get installed, even on a portion of the file that's now logically beyond the file's end.

But, hang on, there's `truncate_inode_pages_range` running in parallel when the file shrinks. And if that hits this folio, it calls `truncate_inode_partial_folio`. That function zeroes the tail of the folio and then tries to SPLIT it.

The crux of the issue is here. If the split succeeds, the split unmaps the PMD, which fixes the problem. Excellent.

But, what if the split *fails*? Then the folio remains in the page cache! And the PMD... also remains. If the PMD is still there, user space accessing the file mapping past the new `i_size` will hit that PMD mapping, see the zeroes, and *not* get the expected SIGBUS signal! The POSIX standard *requires* a SIGBUS for accesses beyond `PAGE_ALIGN(i_size)`.

So, the critical question is: Does `truncate_inode_partial_folio` explicitly unmap the PMD when the split fails? And what happens when `split_huge_page` fails? Is the PMD unmapped then? I need to check if `truncate_inode_partial_folio` does anything to unmap it manually... I'll dive in and see.

    "thought": "**The POSIX SIGBUS Bug in File Mapping with Truncation**

Okay, so here's what I've figured out. `truncate_inode_partial_folio` doesn't unmap the folio if the split fails. It *just* zeroes the partial part. However, this zeroing calls `folio_zero_range`, and potentially, `filemap_map_folio_range` can map the PMD! The problem is this: If the split fails, we can end up with a PMD that covers the now zeroed tail of the file. And that, I realized, is not allowed. `truncate_pagecache` handles the partial folio by zeroing the tail. If it's a large folio that can't be split, it zero-fills the tail pages. Now, here's the kicker: this zero-filled tail means userspace, when accessing the file, *sees* zeroes. Is that POSIX compliant? Yep, it is! POSIX dictates that accessing a memory-mapped file beyond the file's end up to the next PAGE_SIZE boundary will result in zeros. But, accessing *beyond* the PAGE_SIZE boundary of the file's end *should* generate a SIGBUS.

Here's the problem: if the folio is mapped as a PMD, accesses *beyond* the PAGE_SIZE boundary will also yield zeros (because those addresses are within the PMD but now beyond `i_size`), *instead* of generating a SIGBUS! This is a bug! Specifically, a critical bug that breaks POSIX SIGBUS semantics when mapping file-backed large folios without correctly checking `i_size`. Both `filemap_map_pages()` and `finish_fault()` are *supposed* to check `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` before allowing PMD mapping.

The root cause is a stale `file_end` value. If `file_end` is calculated BEFORE a loop (like it is in the current code), then the value might be the old, larger file size. If `filemap_map_folio_range()` uses this outdated `file_end`, it could install a PMD mapping for a large folio that has been partially truncated (e.g., `i_size` is now in the middle of the folio). Then, userspace can access beyond the PAGE_SIZE boundary of `i_size` without getting the SIGBUS.

Let me break down the scenario:

1.  CPU 1: `filemap_map_pages` starts.
2.  CPU 1: `file_end = DIV_ROUND_UP(i_size_read(...), PAGE_SIZE) - 1;` is calculated, which will be the *old* (large) file size.
3.  CPU 2: `ftruncate(fd, smaller_size);` which reduces the file.
4.  CPU 2: `truncate_pagecache` locks the large folio that needs to be truncated.
5.  CPU 2: `truncate_inode_partial_folio` zeroes the tail and attempts to split the folio. The split fails.
6.  CPU 2: unlocks the folio. The large folio remains in the page cache, partially zeroed.
7.  CPU 1: `next_uptodate_folio()` retrieves the folio. It locks it.
8.  CPU 1: `next_uptodate_folio` checks that the folio index is still valid.
9.  CPU 1: `filemap_map_pages` calls `filemap_map_folio_range` with the stale `file_end` value.
10. CPU 1: `filemap_map_folio_range` checks `file_end >= folio_next_index(folio)` which evaluates to TRUE because `file_end` is the stale, large file size, so it maps the folio as a PMD!

The result? Userspace can access beyond `i_size` (the *new* file size), read zeroes, and *not* get the SIGBUS that is required by POSIX, a HUGE FAIL. Because the stale `file_end` fools `filemap_map_folio_range`, the zeroed-out tail is now covered by the PMD!

The patch corrects this by recalculating `file_end` right before the loop, or passing the correct value in another way, and it correctly verifies the `file_end` within `filemap_map_folio_range`. It ensures that `file_end` is updated after the folio is locked.

If truncation zeroes the tail of a large folio and fails to split it, the folio stays in the page cache. Then, `filemap_map_pages` might lock that folio

    "thought": "**Race Condition in `filemap_map_pages` and Truncate Implementation Leading to SIGBUS Violation**

Okay, here's what I've got. It seems there's a potential race condition between `truncate_inode_partial_folio` and `filemap_map_pages` that could lead to a violation of SIGBUS semantics. The core of the problem lies in how `filemap_map_pages` handles a stale `file_end` value after a truncate operation.

My initial thought was, `truncate_inode_partial_folio` seems to do the right thing, by unmapping and zeroing out the relevant portion of the folio when the split fails. It *should* ensure that any subsequent access beyond the new `i_size` will trigger a fault. However, if there's a race with `filemap_map_pages`, things get messy.

Here's the scenario that has me concerned:

1.  `truncate` gets a large folio, locks it, attempts a split (which fails), unmaps it, and zeroes out the tail. Then releases the lock.
2.  `filemap_map_pages` runs *after* the truncate unlocks the folio.

The problem? `filemap_map_pages` calculates `file_end` *before* it acquires the folio lock. If `truncate` shrinks the file size in between these two events, the value of `file_end` becomes stale and larger than the new file size (`i_size`). When `filemap_map_pages` then calls `next_uptodate_folio`, the stale `file_end` is used to decide the extent of the mapping.

This is where things go wrong. Because `file_end` is stale, `filemap_map_folio_range` (or even `filemap_map_pmd`) incorrectly assumes the folio needs to be mapped up to the *old* file end, even though the truncate operation has already unmapped any pages beyond the new `i_size`. While it's looping over the pages, calling `set_pte_range` to establish PTEs for *all* the pages in the folio.

The comment in `filemap_map_pages` explicitly states that it *shouldn't* map across `i_size` to preserve SIGBUS semantics. It calls `filemap_map_pmd` if `file_end` is larger than the folio, and `filemap_map_pmd` establishes a PMD. So the problem is twofold: First, `filemap_map_pages` might call `filemap_map_pmd`, re-establishing a PMD *across* the new `i_size` (violating the SIGBUS semantics). Second, `filemap_map_folio_range` is called, and it sets PTEs for the *entire* folio, which is also a violation.

So, instead of a SIGBUS, userspace would end up reading zeroed memory beyond the intended file size. That's a definite violation. The stale `file_end` value, calculated before the folio lock is acquired, is the culprit here. We have a TOCTOU (Time-Of-Check-To-Time-Of-Use) race. The check of `i_size` is not atomic with the subsequent use in determining the extent of the mapping. This stale value allows `filemap_map_pages` to undo the unmapping done by the truncate operation. Okay, so I've been digging into this code and I think I've found a race condition that could be a real problem. The issue centers around how the kernel handles file truncation and concurrent mapping of file pages. It seems if `file_end` is stale (based on an old `i_size`), there's a window where we can end up mapping memory *beyond* the current file size, breaking the expected SIGBUS behavior on out-of-bounds access.

The core of the problem lies in the interaction between `truncate_inode_partial_folio` (used during truncation) and `filemap_map_pages` (used for mapping file pages). Here's how it plays out: `truncate_inode_partial_folio` might unmap a large folio straddling `i_size`. But if a write access happen afterward to the straddling part, it simply zeros it instead. Then, `filemap_map_pages` can come along and, due to a stale `file_end` value from BEFORE the truncate, think the file is still larger. This stale value is the key!

Because `file_end` is calculated BEFORE the first folio is locked, `file_end` can be based on an *old* `i_size`. If a truncate happens concurrently, shrinking `i_size`, then `next_uptodate_folio` returns the straddling folio, but now `filemap_map_pages` uses this outdated `file_end` for its calculations. Crucially, the code calculates `nr_pages` based on the stale `file_end`, potentially covering the entire large folio, or even, worse, mapping it with a PMD!

The older code was safer, because `file_end` was computed inside a loop after the folio lock was acquired (at least for the first folio).

Now the old value of `file_end` could cause `filemap_map_folio_range` to establish PTEs for the whole large folio, potentially using a PMD, or it may establish PTEs and loop through them. The result is that accesses to pages beyond the *new*, smaller `i_size` won't generate a SIGBUS, as they should, but will rather access the zeroed page. This completely circumvents the SIGBUS protection that `try_folio_split_or_unmap` worked so hard to enforce during truncation.

The fix introduced to avoid an overflow, unfortunately introduced this new vulnerability. This is a classic TOCTOU violation: the code reads `file_end` before acquiring the folio lock, then uses that stale value to make critical mapping decisions. So there's a window where a concurrent truncate can break the logic.

The question then arises: is this exploitable? Yes, as the code will allow a process to write into pages beyond `i_size`, and if the file grows later, the written data might be visible. The worst-case scenario could be writing sensitive information into these beyond-`i_size` pages, and then growing the file later, exposing the information in the now-accessible regions of the file. That would be a serious security concern.
